content_items.title: Why Time Management is Hard with ADHD
content_items.instructions: Read through this before starting the exercises.

content_blocks.heading: Time Blindness
content_blocks.body: People with ADHD often experience 'time blindness' — difficulty sensing how much time has passed or how long tasks will take. This is not laziness. It is a neurological difference in how your brain tracks time.

content_blocks.heading: Why Plans Fall Apart
content_blocks.body: Common traps include underestimating task duration, getting distracted mid-task, forgetting plans that aren't written down, and not building in buffer time.

content_blocks.heading: What You Will Learn in This Module
content_blocks.body: Over 6 steps you will learn to: evaluate your current time management, log how you actually spend time, set goals, make and prioritise lists, estimate time accurately, schedule your week, and build in rewards.

schema.clinician_notes: Normalise time blindness before starting exercises. Avoid framing as a moral failing.

content_items.title: Time Management Evaluation (Table 5.1)
content_items.instructions: For each item below, rate how often you experience this. Be honest — there are no right or wrong answers. This is your baseline. You will repeat this at the end of the module to see how much you have improved.

fields.label: Do you make plans for your time?
Do you write down your plans?
Do you complete everything you had planned to do for a day?
Is your time interrupted by seemingly urgent tasks?
Do you leave things unfinished?
Do you forget what you were supposed to be doing?
Do you arrive on time and prepared for appointments?
Do you know roughly what time it is without checking?
Do you feel that you have wasted your time?

interpretation.0-12: Significant time management difficulties. This module will be very helpful.
interpretation.13-24: Moderate difficulties. Several strategies in this module will make a big difference.
interpretation.25-36: Relatively good time awareness. Focus on the areas where you scored lower.

schema.instructions_for_patient: Score your answers honestly. The total helps us understand where to focus.
schema.clinician_notes: Administer at baseline and repeat at module end. Use lower-scored items to prioritise which exercises to emphasise in sessions.

content_items.title: Diary Activity Log (Table 5.2)
content_items.instructions: For the next week, log what you actually do in each time slot. Start by filling in the last 2 days from memory in your session, then continue for the rest of the week. Be honest — this is for you, not a test.

fields.label: Date
7am – 8am: What did you do?
fields.placeholder: e.g. Asleep, Got up, Had breakfast...

fields.label: 8am – 9am
9am – 10am
10am – 11am
11am – 12pm
12pm – 1pm
1pm – 2pm
2pm – 3pm
3pm – 4pm
4pm – 5pm
5pm – 6pm
6pm – 7pm
7pm – 8pm
8pm – 9pm
9pm – 10pm
10pm onwards
Did you take your medication today?
Looking at your day — what surprised you most about how you spent your time?
fields.placeholder: Notice patterns, wasted time, best hours...

schema.instructions_for_patient: Do one log per day for 7 days. It only takes 3 minutes at the end of each day.
schema.clinician_notes: Review the completed log in session. Look for: wasted time patterns, best focus hours, medication timing effects, chaotic vs structured days. Use to guide the time plan exercise.

content_items.title: Six Steps to Making a Time Plan (Table 5.3)
content_items.instructions: Follow these 6 steps with your therapist to build your first weekly time plan. You will use this skill every week going forward.

fields.label: Step 1 — Set Goals: What do you need to achieve this week?
fields.placeholder: List 3-5 things you must get done this week...

fields.label: Step 2 — Make Lists: Break each goal into specific tasks
fields.placeholder: e.g. Goal: 'Pay bills' → Tasks: find bills, log into bank, transfer money, take screenshot...

fields.label: Step 3 — Prioritise: Rate each task 0 (least important) to 4 (most important)
fields.placeholder: Go through your list and add a priority number next to each task...

fields.label: Step 4 — Estimate Time: How long will each task take? (be honest)
fields.placeholder: Add time estimates. Tip: most things take 2x longer than you think.

fields.label: Step 5 — Schedule: When this week will you do each task?
fields.placeholder: Assign tasks to specific days and time slots. Include contingency time.

fields.label: Step 6 — Reward System: What will you give yourself for completing priority tasks?
fields.placeholder: e.g. Watch a show after finishing the bills. Order food after completing job applications.

schema.instructions_for_patient: Do this in your session first. Then repeat on your own every Sunday for the coming week.
schema.clinician_notes: Work through all 6 steps together in session. Emphasise Step 6 — reward systems are often omitted but critical for ADHD motivation. Revisit the priority task list mid-week if possible.

content_items.title: Priority Task List (Table 5.5)
content_items.instructions: List all your tasks for the week, then rate each one from 0 (least important) to 4 (most important). This helps you focus on what actually matters.

fields.label: List your tasks and rate their priority (0-4)

schema.instructions_for_patient: Review and update this list every few days — priorities change.
schema.clinician_notes: Priorities are dynamic. Teach the patient to review this regularly rather than treating it as a fixed list.

content_items.title: Time Estimation Exercise (Table 5.6)
content_items.instructions: People with ADHD often underestimate how long things take. This exercise helps calibrate your time sense. Estimate how long each task will take, then actually time yourself doing it.

fields.label: For each activity, write your estimate, then time yourself

sub_fields.label: Activity
sub_fields.placeholder: e.g. Have a shower, Make tea, Read one article...

prefill_suggestions: Have a shower and dry off
Get ready to leave the house

fields.label: What did you notice? Were you consistently over or under-estimating?

schema.instructions_for_patient: Do the estimation column now. Fill in the actual column after you do each activity this week.
schema.clinician_notes: This is a behavioural experiment. Review results together. Most ADHD patients significantly underestimate. Use this to build a personal 'correction factor' into future time plans.

content_items.title: Evaluating the Importance of Punctuality (Table 5.9)
content_items.instructions: Being late affects more than just you. This exercise helps you connect lateness to real consequences — which builds motivation to change.

fields.label: List situations where being on time matters to you

sub_fields.label: Situation
sub_fields.placeholder: e.g. Arriving at work, Picking up kids...

sub_fields.label: What actually happens when you are late?

fields.label: Which situation is most important for you to be on time for and why?

schema.instructions_for_patient: Being honest here will help you prioritise which situations to plan around first.
schema.clinician_notes: This exercise shifts punctuality from a vague goal to a concrete, consequence-linked priority. Use the highest-rated situations as anchors for planning exercises.
